text: When Jacob learned there was grain in Egypt, he asked his sons why they just stared at each other.
He said, I've heard there is grain in Egypt; go down and buy some so we may live and not die.
So ten of Joseph's brothers went down to buy grain from Egypt.
But Jacob did not send Benjamin, Joseph's brother, fearing harm might come to him.
Israel's sons came to buy grain along with others, since the famine was in Canaan too.
Now Joseph was governor of the land and sold grain to all its people; his brothers came and bowed down to him with their faces to the ground.
As soon as Joseph saw his brothers, he recognized them but treated them as strangers and spoke harshly, asking where they came from; they said, from Canaan, to buy food.
Joseph recognized his brothers, but they did not recognize him.
He remembered his dreams about them and said, you are spies, come to see where our land is unprotected.
No, my lord, they said, your servants have come to buy food.
We are all the sons of one man; your servants are honest men, not spies.
No, he said, you have come to see where our land is unprotected.
They said, we are twelve brothers, sons of one man in Canaan; the youngest is with our father, and one is no more.
Joseph said, it is just as I said: you are spies.
This is how you'll be tested: unless your youngest brother comes here, you will not leave, as surely as Pharaoh lives.
Send one to get your brother; the rest of you will be kept in prison, so your words can be tested, whether you're telling the truth.
He put them all in custody for three days.
On the third day Joseph said, do this and you'll live, for I fear God.
If you are honest men, let one brother stay in prison while the rest take grain to relieve your starving households.
Bring your youngest brother to me, so your words may be verified and you will not die; they agreed.
They said to each other, surely we are being punished for our brother, for we saw his anguish and did not listen; that's why this distress has come.
Reuben said, didn't I tell you not to sin against the boy? But you wouldn't listen; now we must account for his blood.
They did not know Joseph understood them, since he had spoken through an interpreter.
Joseph turned away and wept, then returned and spoke to them, taking Simeon and binding him before their eyes.
Joseph gave orders to fill their bags with grain, return each man's silver into his sack, and give them provisions for the journey.
They loaded their grain on their donkeys and left.
At the lodging place one of them opened his sack to feed his donkey and saw his silver in the mouth of his sack.
He said to his brothers, my silver has been returned; here it is in my sack! Their hearts sank, and they turned trembling to each other, saying what has God done to us?
When they came to Jacob their father in Canaan, they told him all that had happened.
The man who is lord over the land spoke harshly to us and treated us as spies.
But we told him we are honest men, not spies.
We are twelve brothers, sons of one father; one is no more, and the youngest is with our father in Canaan.
He said, this is how I'll know you're honest: leave one brother with me, take food, and go.
Bring your youngest brother to me, and I'll know you're honest men, not spies; then I'll give your brother back and you can trade in the land.
As they emptied their sacks, each man's bundle of silver was there; seeing this, they and their father were frightened.
Jacob said, you have deprived me of my children; Joseph is no more, Simeon is no more, and now you want to take Benjamin too; everything is against me.
Reuben said to his father, put Benjamin in my care, and I'll bring him back; you may kill my two sons if I fail.
Jacob said, my son will not go down with you; his brother is dead, and he is the only one left; if harm comes to him, you would bring my gray head down to the grave in sorrow.
